title: B1 anthocyanin mapping benchmark (DEFERRED)
text:
ImportantSTATUS: DEFERRED (B2.4)



The B1 phenotype table + NIL panel are not ready (per user). This note plans the pipeline abstractly now; it becomes a real analysis once the B1 phenotype and panel are located, otherwise it stays a “future application” paragraph in the Discussion.



Planned pipeline (abstract)

Project decoded ancestry → mapping genotype. Convert each caller’s REF/HET/ALT segments to a segment/dosage genotype — the mapping unit implied by the zygosity-identifiability ceiling (memory snp50k-zygosity-identifiability, gatk-vs-posterior-genotype-calling).
Map the B1 QTL (anthocyanin) per (caller × source) call set.
Compare mapping power/resolution — QTL detection, support-interval width, effect-size stability — across callers and sequencing modalities.



Why it matters
This is the downstream payoff: do the simulation-calibrated calls (B2.2) and the cheaper modality (BrB/skim vs target-seq) recover the same QTL at comparable resolution? A “yes” closes the loop from calibration → concordance → application.


Inputs still needed

B1 anthocyanin phenotype table (sample → phenotype).
The NIL panel genotyped for B1 (overlap with the paired cohort in B2.3).

title: The nilHMM caller family: one duration-aware HMM, swappable emissions
text:
Scope. This note is the methods backbone of the paper (Oxford Bioinformatics §2.1–2.2). It describes the one engine and the four callers that ride on it, and shows each running through the installed nilHMM package — establishing the (data, params) → calls contract every later benchmark relies on. API details live in the package’s pkgdown site (https://sawers-rellan-labs.github.io/nilhmm/); this note cites it rather than restating signatures.

Emits (paper): Figure F1 (engine + caller-family schematic). Ports/refactors the zealtiger notes generative_vs_discriminative_callers, emission_by_depth_regime, genotype_likelihoods_and_hmm, snp50k_genotype_identifiability, and the ATLAS section of nilhmm_sanity_check_paint, rewritten onto library(nilHMM).



Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(nilHMM)
  library(data.table)
  library(ggplot2)
})
# shared analysis helpers (staging / metrics / plotting)
for (f in list.files(here::here("R"), "\\.R$", full.names = TRUE)) source(f)



1. The shared engine
A single duration-aware 3-state (REF / HET / ALT) HMM. Two axes are swappable and define each caller; everything else (Viterbi decode, sticky transitions, design start-priors) is shared:

Emission — how a marker’s data scores against each state:

count / BetaBinomial (emission_count()) — allele counts at SNP sites.
gt / categorical genotype-confusion (emission_gt()) — hard genotypes.

Duration / rigidity — how long the chain stays in a state: duration_geometric(), duration_rigidity(), duration_hsmm().

Design start-priors come from design_priors("BC2S2" | "BC2S3") (donor allele frequency and heterozygosity expected from the pedigree). The data-agnostic contract is call_ancestry(data, caller, design, params...) → segment table.


2. The four callers










Caller
Emission
Duration
Typical input
Lineage




nNIL
count / BetaBinom
geometric
SNP-panel allele counts
Holland nNIL


RTIGER
count / BetaBinom
rigidity (r)
SNP-panel allele counts
RTIGER (port)


binhmm
Gaussian on bins
sticky trans.
per-bin ALT_FREQ (dense skim)
nilhifi


ATLAS
gt / categorical
geometric
competitive gene-level GT counts
GOOGA-shadow



The Julia-free RTIGER port (memory rtiger-fork) is what lets all four share one R + Rcpp engine. Each caller gets a §2.2 subsection in the manuscript; the prose is drafted in the RILAB LaTeX template in the separate manuscript repo (see the README section→note→figure map).


3. Smoke test — every caller returns a valid segment table
This chunk proves the pipeline: the same engine, four callers, each producing a common-schema segment table. It renders off-mount by falling back to a tiny bundled fixture when the real count files are absent.


Show code
# TODO: point at a small bundled fixture under data/ once the manifest (B3) is
# frozen. Until then this documents the intended call surface.
demo_calls <- function(path, ...) {
  if (!file.exists(path)) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  as_common_schema(call_ancestry(read_counts(path), ...),
    source = "demo", name = basename(path)
  )
}
# nNIL:   demo_calls(f, caller = "nnil",   design = "BC2S2", rrate = 1e-4, err = 0.01)
# RTIGER: demo_calls(f, caller = "rtiger", rigidity = 5L)
cat("nilHMM", as.character(packageVersion("nilHMM")), "loaded; engine ready.\n")


nilHMM 0.1.0 loaded; engine ready.




4. What each caller is for

nNIL — the count baseline; Holland’s near-isogenic-line caller.
RTIGER — rigidity replaces a free geometric duration; robust at low depth.
binhmm — anchors REF at the coverage floor on dense per-bin ALT_FREQ; the only track that keeps the full ~27.6 M-SNP density (skim only).
ATLAS — competitive transcript mapping → hard GT → categorical emission; expression-agnostic, built for BRB-seq (RNA), where a count model is confounded by expression and allele-specific expression.

See simulation-calibration.qmd for how each caller’s parameters are calibrated from simulations, and source-method-comparison.qmd for the cross-modality benchmark.

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
text:
What this is. A self-contained, hard-proven record of the marker-thinning step that builds the JLM marker framework (data/teonam/tassel/geno.hmp.txt, 9063 markers). Every claim below is reproduced live from the primary data — no approximations, no “close enough.” The three things this notebook nails down:
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
suppressMessages(devtools::load_all("/Users/fvrodriguez/repos/nilhmm", quiet = TRUE))
theme_set(theme_bw(base_size = 12))
THR <- 0.1 # cM thinning threshold

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
section: The two published methods (Chen et al. 2019)
text:
The two published methods (Chen et al. 2019)
Chen 2019 (Genetics 213:1065–1078) used two separate marker sets for its two mapping methods. Quoting the Methods verbatim:

“Because stepwise regression is computationally intensive, we thinned SNPs within 0.1 cM. We finally obtained 4578 markers for subsequent joint linkage analysis.” — (JLM marker set)


“…a total of 118,838 SNPs were kept and used for GWAS. GWAS was performed using a linear mixed model accounting for population structure (Q) and kinship matrix (K)…” — (GWAS marker set, a different pipeline)

This notebook concerns the JLM set only (the 4578-marker analogue). The paper’s composite genetic map is 1540 cM; ours (native TeoNAM est.map cM) is ~1561 cM — so map length is essentially Chen’s, and the count difference is instead driven by how finely the native map resolves markers into 0.1-cM bins, as we show below.

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
text:
The thinning as implemented here
The pipeline (scripts/teonam_reproduce_fastindep.R, and originally the lost inline prep this notebook now replaces) does:

take every marker in the native TeoNAM map (teonam_v5_native.tsv, native est.map cM);
dedup to unique cM per chromosome (the densify grid: !duplicated(cm));
run select_independent on the per-chromosome cM-distance matrix (|cm_i − cm_j|), with sense = "distance", threshold = 0.1, n_runs = 1 (deterministic greedy, documented bit-identical to the FastIndep CLI).

The subtlety: select_independent is an LD-pruning tool — normally you hand it a genotype matrix and it prunes on r². Here it was handed a distance matrix, so it prunes on genetic distance instead. That choice is what we verify and dissect.

# target: the markers actually in the JLM input on disk
target <- fread(here("data/teonam/tassel/geno.hmp.txt"), select = "rs#")[["rs#"]]

# candidate pool: native map (placed markers), unique-cM dedup per chromosome
mc <- fread(here("data/teonam/teonam_v5_native.tsv"))[!is.na(cm)]
setnames(mc, "chr_v5", "chr")
pool <- mc[order(chr, cm)][, .SD[!duplicated(cm)], by = chr]

# run the REAL FastIndep greedy on the cM-distance matrix, per chromosome
thin_distance <- function(m) { # m: data.table(marker, cm)
  m <- m[order(cm)]
  D <- abs(outer(m$cm, m$cm, `-`))
  dimnames(D) <- list(m$marker, m$marker)
  as.character(select_independent(D,
    threshold = THR, n_runs = 1L,
    sense = "distance", max_markers = nrow(D) + 1L
  ))
}
sel <- unlist(lapply(
  sort(unique(pool$chr)),
  function(ch) thin_distance(pool[chr == ch, .(marker, cm)])
))


Proof of exact reproduction

proof <- data.table(
  metric = c(
    "selected", "target (on disk)", "in both",
    "selected-only", "target-only", "Jaccard", "setequal"
  ),
  value = c(
    length(sel), length(target), length(intersect(sel, target)),
    length(setdiff(sel, target)), length(setdiff(target, sel)),
    round(length(intersect(sel, target)) / length(union(sel, target)), 4),
    as.integer(setequal(sel, target))
  )
)
knitr::kable(proof, caption = "FastIndep on the cM-distance pool vs the JLM file on disk")


FastIndep on the cM-distance pool vs the JLM file on disk


metric
value




selected
9063


target (on disk)
9063


in both
9063


selected-only
0


target-only
0


Jaccard
1


setequal
1




stopifnot(setequal(sel, target)) # the notebook fails to render if this is not exact

setequal = 1, Jaccard = 1.000: the thinning is reproduced bit-for-bit. This is the ground truth the rest of the notebook interprets. (For the record: running the same algorithm on the raw marker_info pool — without the unique-cM dedup — gives 6056 markers with 51 mismatches; the dedup step is load-bearing, and pinning that down is exactly why “99% overlap” is not “reproduced.”)

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
section: Diagnostic 1 — the retained set has a hard 0.1 cM floor
text:
Diagnostic 1 — the retained set has a hard 0.1 cM floor
If the thinning pruned on distance, no two retained markers can sit closer than 0.1 cM. If it had pruned on r², close markers in low LD would survive. The gap distribution decides it.

km <- merge(data.table(marker = target), mc[, .(marker, chr, cm)], by = "marker")[order(chr, cm)]
gaps <- km[, .(gap = diff(cm)), by = chr]$gap
gtab <- data.table(
  stat = c("min", "1%", "5%", "median", "% < 0.10 cM", "% < 0.05 cM"),
  cM = c(
    round(quantile(gaps, c(0, .01, .05, .5)), 4),
    round(100 * mean(gaps < 0.10), 2), round(100 * mean(gaps < 0.05), 2)
  )
)
knitr::kable(gtab, caption = "Adjacent cM-gap between retained JLM markers")


Adjacent cM-gap between retained JLM markers


stat
cM




min
0.1000


1%
0.1010


5%
0.1050


median
0.1509


% < 0.10 cM
0.0000


% < 0.05 cM
0.0000




ggplot(data.table(gap = gaps[gaps < 1]), aes(gap)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01, fill = "grey30", boundary = 0) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0.1, colour = "red", linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    x = "adjacent cM gap between retained markers", y = "count",
    title = "Hard floor at exactly 0.10 cM (red) — the signature of a distance thin",
    subtitle = "0% of neighbours fall below 0.10 cM; an r² prune could not produce this floor"
  )








The minimum gap is exactly 0.10 cM and nothing is below it — the unambiguous fingerprint of a cM-distance thin, not r².

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
section: Diagnostic 2 — distance ≠ r²: proven, not asserted
text:
Diagnostic 2 — distance ≠ r²: proven, not asserted
To show the consequence of feeding FastIndep cM distances instead of genotype r², we run both on the same chromosome-1 window and compare the sets they keep. The genotypes are densified live here from the raw family files by step-interpolation — the same operation as scripts/teonam_stam_gwas_scan.R::densify_chr — so this notebook depends on no pre-saved intermediate (.rds) of unknown provenance; only primary data (W22TIL*_genotype.csv + the native map).

# --- densify a chr1 window from the RAW family genotypes (step-interpolation) ---
fams <- c(
  TIL01 = "W22TIL01_genotype.csv", TIL03 = "W22TIL03_genotype.csv",
  TIL11 = "W22TIL11_genotype.csv", TIL14 = "W22TIL14_genotype.csv",
  TIL25 = "W22TIL25_genotype.csv"
)
cm_by <- setNames(mc$cm, mc$marker)
mch <- mc[chr == 1 & cm >= 20 & cm <= 45][order(cm)]
tgt <- mch[!duplicated(cm)] # unique-cM target grid (the window)
tgt_df <- data.frame(chr = 1, cm = tgt$cm)
win <- data.table(marker = tgt$marker, cm = tgt$cm)

densify_fam <- function(fam) {
  fp <- here("data/teonam", fams[fam])
  hdr <- names(fread(fp, nrows = 0))
  cols <- intersect(hdr, mch$marker) # this family's markers in the window
  g <- fread(fp, select = c(hdr[1], cols))
  g <- g[!duplicated(g[[1]])]
  keys <- paste0(fam, sub("^.*Line_", "", g[[1]])) # e.g. TIL01A001
  obs <- data.frame(marker = cols, cm = cm_by[cols])
  obs <- obs[order(obs$cm), ]
  obs <- obs[!duplicated(obs$cm), ]
  geno <- t(as.matrix(g[, obs$marker, with = FALSE]))
  storage.mode(geno) <- "double"
  dn <- interpolate_genotype(geno, data.frame(chr = 1, cm = obs$cm), tgt_df, mode = "step")
  colnames(dn) <- keys
  dn
}
Gw <- do.call(cbind, lapply(names(fams), densify_fam))
rownames(Gw) <- tgt$marker

# (a) distance thin — what the pipeline did
selD <- thin_distance(win[, .(marker, cm)])

# (b) r² thin — what FastIndep is designed for — swept across thresholds
gap_of <- function(ids) {
  k <- win[marker %in% ids][order(cm)]
  diff(k$cm)
}
r2_thin <- function(t) {
  as.character(
    select_independent(Gw,
      threshold = t, method = "r2",
      n_runs = 1L, max_markers = nrow(Gw) + 1L
    )
  )
}

rows <- list(data.table(
  thinning = "cM-distance (t=0.1)", n_kept = length(selD),
  min_gap_cM = round(min(gap_of(selD)), 4),
  median_gap_cM = round(median(gap_of(selD)), 3)
))
for (t in c(0.2, 0.5, 0.9, 0.99)) {
  s <- r2_thin(t)
  g <- gap_of(s)
  rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- data.table(
    thinning = sprintf("r² LD (t=%.2f)", t), n_kept = length(s),
    min_gap_cM = round(min(g), 4), median_gap_cM = round(median(g), 3)
  )
}
knitr::kable(rbindlist(rows),
  caption =
    "Chr1 window (866 markers over 24.3 cM): cM-distance vs r² thinning"
)


Chr1 window (866 markers over 24.3 cM): cM-distance vs r² thinning


thinning
n_kept
min_gap_cM
median_gap_cM




cM-distance (t=0.1)

0.148


r² LD (t=0.20)

r² LD (t=0.50)

12.389


r² LD (t=0.90)
15

r² LD (t=0.99)
143

0.168






plt <- rbind(
  data.table(cm = win[marker %in% selD]$cm, method = "cM-distance (t=0.1)"),
  data.table(cm = win[marker %in% r2_thin(0.5)]$cm, method = "r² LD (t=0.50)"),
  data.table(cm = win[marker %in% r2_thin(0.99)]$cm, method = "r² LD (t=0.99)")
)
plt[, method := factor(method, levels = c("cM-distance (t=0.1)", "r² LD (t=0.50)", "r² LD (t=0.99)"))]
ggplot(plt, aes(cm, method, colour = method)) +
  geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.55) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = c("steelblue4", "firebrick", "darkorange"), guide = "none") +
  labs(
    x = "cM (chr 1)", y = NULL,
    title = "Same window, three thinnings — r² tracks LD (threshold-dominated); distance tracks spacing"
  )








The two are different operations, and the r² set is not even monotonic in spacing — it tracks LD, and TeoNAM ancestry LD is long-range. At r² ≥ 0.2 the entire 24 cM window collapses to ~2 markers (essentially one ancestry block); only at a very strict r² ≥ 0.99 does r² pruning become dense enough to admit markers < 0.1 cM apart (min gap 0.000 cM — pairs the distance thin forbids). The cM-distance thin (99 markers, hard 0.10 cM floor) sits in neither regime: it is indifferent to LD. That is the concrete consequence of feeding FastIndep a cM matrix — the JLM framework guarantees genetic spacing but does no LD decorrelation, so many retained neighbours remain in near-perfect LD (collinear, redundant columns for the stepwise model).

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
section: The count: 9063 vs 4578, decomposed to the marker
text:
The count: 9063 vs 4578, decomposed to the marker
Both we and Chen ran a 0.1 cM thin, so the algorithm is not the source of the difference. 9063 / 4578 ≈ 1.98 decomposes into map length and bin saturation (the fraction of 0.1-cM bins that contain ≥1 marker) — but the two factors are now far from equal: length is ~1.0 (native ≈ Chen), so bin saturation (~2.0) carries essentially the whole difference:

maplen_ours <- mc[, max(cm) - min(cm), by = chr][, sum(V1)]
maplen_paper <- 1540
n_ours <- length(target)
n_paper <- 4578
sat_ours <- n_ours / (maplen_ours / 0.1) # occupied 0.1-cM bins / total bins
sat_paper <- n_paper / (maplen_paper / 0.1)
decomp <- data.table(
  factor = c(
    "map length (cM)", "0.1-cM bins available", "markers kept",
    "bin saturation", "— length ratio", "— saturation ratio",
    "— product (predicted)", "— observed ratio"
  ),
  ours = c(
    round(maplen_ours), round(maplen_ours / 0.1), n_ours, round(sat_ours, 3),
    NA, NA, NA, NA
  ),
  paper = c(
    maplen_paper, round(maplen_paper / 0.1), n_paper, round(sat_paper, 3),
    NA, NA, NA, NA
  ),
  ratio = c(
    NA, NA, NA, NA,
    round(maplen_ours / maplen_paper, 3), round(sat_ours / sat_paper, 3),
    round((maplen_ours / maplen_paper) * (sat_ours / sat_paper), 3),
    round(n_ours / n_paper, 3)
  )
)
knitr::kable(decomp,
  caption =
    "Map length × bin saturation reproduces the marker-count ratio"
)


Map length × bin saturation reproduces the marker-count ratio

The predicted product matches the observed 9063/4578 ratio: with the native map at ~Chen’s length, the extra markers come almost entirely from bin saturation — the native est.map resolves ~2× the fraction of 0.1-cM bins that Chen’s map did — not from map length and not a thinning-method artifact.

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
section: The two published methods (Chen et al. 2019)
text:
The two published methods (Chen et al. 2019)
Chen 2019 (Genetics 213:1065–1078) used two separate marker sets for its two mapping methods. Quoting the Methods verbatim:

sel <- unlist(lapply(

  function(ch) thin_distance(pool[chr == ch, .(marker, cm)])

Proof of exact reproduction

  metric = c(
    "selected", "target (on disk)", "in both",
    "selected-only", "target-only", "Jaccard", "setequal"

  value = c(
    length(sel), length(target), length(intersect(sel, target)),

stopifnot(setequal(sel, target)) # the notebook fails to render if this is not exact

(For the record: running the same algorithm on the raw marker_info pool — without the unique-cM dedup — gives 6056 markers with 51 mismatches; the dedup step is load-bearing, and pinning that down is exactly why “99% overlap” is not “reproduced.”)

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
section: Diagnostic 1 — the retained set has a hard 0.1 cM floor
text:
Diagnostic 1 — the retained set has a hard 0.1 cM floor
If the thinning pruned on distance, no two retained markers can sit closer than 0.1 cM. If it had pruned on r², close markers in low LD would survive. The gap distribution decides it.
```{r gap-floor, fig.width=8, fig.height=3.4} km <- merge(data.table(marker = target), mc[, .(marker, chr, cm)], by = “marker”)[order(chr, cm)] gaps <- km[, .(gap = diff(cm)), by = chr]$gap gtab <- data.table( stat = c(“min”, “1%”, “5%”, “median”, “% < 0.10 cM”, “% < 0.05 cM”), cM = c( round(quantile(gaps, c(0, .01, .05, .5)), 4), round(100 * mean(gaps < 0.10), 2), round(100 * mean(gaps < 0.05), 2) ) ) knitr::kable(gtab, caption = “Adjacent cM-gap between retained JLM markers”)
ggplot(data.table(gap = gaps[gaps < 1]), aes(gap)) + geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.01, fill = “grey30”, boundary = 0) + geom_vline(xintercept = 0.1, colour = “red”, linetype = “dashed”) + labs( x = “adjacent cM gap between retained markers”, y = “count”, title = “Hard floor at exactly 0.10 cM (red) — the signature of a distance thin”, subtitle = “0% of neighbours fall below 0.10 cM; an r² prune could not produce this floor” )

The minimum gap is exactly 0.10 cM and **nothing** is below it — the unambiguous
fingerprint of a cM-distance thin, not r².

## Diagnostic 2 — distance ≠ r²: proven, not asserted

To show the *consequence* of feeding FastIndep cM distances instead of genotype
r², we run **both** on the same chromosome-1 window and compare the sets they keep.
The genotypes are **densified live here from the raw family files** by
step-interpolation — the same operation as `scripts/teonam_stam_gwas_scan.R::densify_chr`
— so this notebook depends on **no pre-saved intermediate** (`.rds`) of unknown
provenance; only primary data (`W22TIL*_genotype.csv` + the native map).

```{r rsq-contrast, fig.width=8, fig.height=4}
# --- densify a chr1 window from the RAW family genotypes (step-interpolation) ---
fams <- c(
  TIL01 = "W22TIL01_genotype.csv", TIL03 = "W22TIL03_genotype.csv",
  TIL11 = "W22TIL11_genotype.csv", TIL14 = "W22TIL14_genotype.csv",
  TIL25 = "W22TIL25_genotype.csv"
)
cm_by <- setNames(mc$cm, mc$marker)
mch <- mc[chr == 1 & cm >= 20 & cm <= 45][order(cm)]
tgt <- mch[!duplicated(cm)] # unique-cM target grid (the window)
tgt_df <- data.frame(chr = 1, cm = tgt$cm)
win <- data.table(marker = tgt$marker, cm = tgt$cm)

densify_fam <- function(fam) {
  fp <- here("data/teonam", fams[fam])
  hdr <- names(fread(fp, nrows = 0))
  cols <- intersect(hdr, mch$marker) # this family's markers in the window
  g <- fread(fp, select = c(hdr[1], cols))
  g <- g[!duplicated(g[[1]])]
  keys <- paste0(fam, sub("^.*Line_", "", g[[1]])) # e.g. TIL01A001
  obs <- data.frame(marker = cols, cm = cm_by[cols])
  obs <- obs[order(obs$cm), ]
  obs <- obs[!duplicated(obs$cm), ]
  geno <- t(as.matrix(g[, obs$marker, with = FALSE]))
  storage.mode(geno) <- "double"
  dn <- interpolate_genotype(geno, data.frame(chr = 1, cm = obs$cm), tgt_df, mode = "step")
  colnames(dn) <- keys
  dn
}
Gw <- do.call(cbind, lapply(names(fams), densify_fam))
rownames(Gw) <- tgt$marker

# (a) distance thin — what the pipeline did
selD <- thin_distance(win[, .(marker, cm)])

# (b) r² thin — what FastIndep is designed for — swept across thresholds
gap_of <- function(ids) {
  k <- win[marker %in% ids][order(cm)]
  diff(k$cm)
}
r2_thin <- function(t) {
  as.character(
    select_independent(Gw,
      threshold = t, method = "r2",
      n_runs = 1L, max_markers = nrow(Gw) + 1L
    )
  )
}

rows <- list(data.table(
  thinning = "cM-distance (t=0.1)", n_kept = length(selD),
  min_gap_cM = round(min(gap_of(selD)), 4),
  median_gap_cM = round(median(gap_of(selD)), 3)
))
for (t in c(0.2, 0.5, 0.9, 0.99)) {
  s <- r2_thin(t)
  g <- gap_of(s)
  rows[[length(rows) + 1]] <- data.table(
    thinning = sprintf("r² LD (t=%.2f)", t), n_kept = length(s),
    min_gap_cM = round(min(g), 4), median_gap_cM = round(median(g), 3)
  )
}
knitr::kable(rbindlist(rows),
  caption =
    "Chr1 window (866 markers over 24.3 cM): cM-distance vs r² thinning"
)
{r rsq-contrast-plot, fig.width=8, fig.height=2.8} plt <- rbind(   data.table(cm = win[marker %in% selD]$cm, method = "cM-distance (t=0.1)"),   data.table(cm = win[marker %in% r2_thin(0.5)]$cm, method = "r² LD (t=0.50)"),   data.table(cm = win[marker %in% r2_thin(0.99)]$cm, method = "r² LD (t=0.99)") ) plt[, method := factor(method, levels = c("cM-distance (t=0.1)", "r² LD (t=0.50)", "r² LD (t=0.99)"))] ggplot(plt, aes(cm, method, colour = method)) +   geom_point(size = 0.7, alpha = 0.55) +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("steelblue4", "firebrick", "darkorange"), guide = "none") +   labs(     x = "cM (chr 1)", y = NULL,     title = "Same window, three thinnings — r² tracks LD (threshold-dominated); distance tracks spacing"   )
The two are different operations, and the r² set is not even monotonic in spacing — it tracks LD, and TeoNAM ancestry LD is long-range. At r² ≥ 0.2 the entire 24 cM window collapses to ~2 markers (essentially one ancestry block); only at a very strict r² ≥ 0.99 does r² pruning become dense enough to admit markers < 0.1 cM apart (min gap 0.000 cM — pairs the distance thin forbids). The cM-distance thin (99 markers, hard 0.10 cM floor) sits in neither regime: it is indifferent to LD. That is the concrete consequence of feeding FastIndep a cM matrix — the JLM framework guarantees genetic spacing but does no LD decorrelation, so many retained neighbours remain in near-perfect LD (collinear, redundant columns for the stepwise model).

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
section: The count: 9063 vs 4578, decomposed to the marker
text:
The count: 9063 vs 4578, decomposed to the marker
Both we and Chen ran a 0.1 cM thin, so the algorithm is not the source of the difference. 9063 / 4578 ≈ 1.98 decomposes into map length and bin saturation (the fraction of 0.1-cM bins that contain ≥1 marker) — but the two factors are now far from equal: length is ~1.0 (native ≈ Chen), so bin saturation (~2.0) carries essentially the whole difference:
maplen_ours <- mc[, max(cm) - min(cm), by = chr][, sum(V1)]
maplen_paper <- 1540
n_ours <- length(target)
n_paper <- 4578
sat_ours <- n_ours / (maplen_ours / 0.1) # occupied 0.1-cM bins / total bins
sat_paper <- n_paper / (maplen_paper / 0.1)
decomp <- data.table(
  factor = c(
    "map length (cM)", "0.1-cM bins available", "markers kept",
    "bin saturation", "— length ratio", "— saturation ratio",
    "— product (predicted)", "— observed ratio"
  ),
  ours = c(
    round(maplen_ours), round(maplen_ours / 0.1), n_ours, round(sat_ours, 3),
    NA, NA, NA, NA
  ),
  paper = c(
    maplen_paper, round(maplen_paper / 0.1), n_paper, round(sat_paper, 3),
    NA, NA, NA, NA
  ),
  ratio = c(
    NA, NA, NA, NA,
    round(maplen_ours / maplen_paper, 3), round(sat_ours / sat_paper, 3),
    round((maplen_ours / maplen_paper) * (sat_ours / sat_paper), 3),
    round(n_ours / n_paper, 3)
  )
)
knitr::kable(decomp,
  caption =
    "Map length × bin saturation reproduces the marker-count ratio"
)
The predicted product matches the observed 9063/4578 ratio: with the native map at ~Chen’s length, the extra markers come almost entirely from bin saturation — the native est.map resolves ~2× the fraction of 0.1-cM bins that Chen’s map did — not from map length and not a thinning-method artifact.

title: Marker thinning for joint-linkage mapping — provenance & proof
text:
Conclusions

The JLM marker framework was built by nilHMM::select_independent on a cM-distance matrix at 0.1 cM (unique-cM dedup pool). Reproduced exactly (Jaccard = 1.000); the render aborts if that ever stops holding.
Feeding FastIndep cM distances turns it into a 0.1 cM minimum-spacing ruler; it performs no LD pruning. Diagnostic 2 proves the distance and r² thinnings keep materially different marker sets.
The 9063 vs 4578 difference is a deterministic property of the native map’s higher bin saturation at essentially Chen’s map length (length × saturation), fully decomposed above — nothing unexplained.

Implication for the design. The r² path is the LD-aware option in principle, but Diagnostic 2 shows it is not a drop-in here: because ancestry LD is long-range, r² pruning is extremely threshold-sensitive (t = 0.5 → a handful of markers per chromosome; only t ≈ 0.99 keeps a workable dense set). The cM-distance thin is the pragmatic choice that matches Chen’s operation (a 0.1 cM thin), at the documented cost of retaining LD-redundant neighbours. Both are defensible; this notebook records which one is in force — a 0.1 cM genetic-distance ruler — so downstream QTL results are read with the right framework in mind.

:::

title: nilHMM sanity check paint: source × caller tracks, one engine, no Julia
text:
Context. This is the cross-track sanity check for the unified nilHMM engine, where every caller runs through one duration-aware 3-state (REF/HET/ALT) HMM with the emission as the only swappable piece. We paint the same vary-skim coverage-sweep NILs (plus a clean B73 control) and eyeball whether the independent tracks agree on where the donor blocks are, and whether the control stays REF. Memory: wideseq-chrpainting-code, generative_vs_discriminative_callers, cassini-googa-caller, method-naming-source-caller, skim-count-caller-calibration, rtiger-betabinomial-cost.
The six tracks are not six callers. Each track is a combination of a data source and a nilHMM caller method (naming convention <DataSource>-<Caller>, source first — memory method-naming-source-caller). There are only two data sources and four distinct callers; Skim and BRB each run three tracks and share the nNIL and RTIGER callers:

Data sources (2): Skim = low-coverage WGS; BrB = 3′ RNA-seq (BRB-seq).
Callers (4 distinct): binhmm (Gaussian on per-bin ALT_FREQ), nNIL (count / BetaBinomial HMM), RTIGER (Julia-free port), ATLAS (GOOGA-shadow gene-level GT).

The tracks are ordered first by data source, then by caller (all three Skim tracks, then all three BRB tracks):










#
Track
Data source
nilHMM caller
Input marker set




1
Skim-binhmm
Skim (low-cov WGS)
binhmm — 3-state Gaussian on per-bin ALT_FREQ, REF anchored at the floor
dense ~27.6 M-SNP set — per-1 Mb ALT_FREQ bin_genotypes from GATK CollectAllelicCounts at the full teosinte-informative wideseq positions (mount, bzeaseq/ancestry)


2
Skim-nNIL
Skim (low-cov WGS)
nNIL — count / BetaBinomial HMM (rrate=1e-4, err=0.01)
50K panel counts (thinned SNP50K)


3
Skim-rtiger
Skim (low-cov WGS)
RTIGER port (rigidity=5)
50K panel counts (thinned SNP50K)


4
BrB-nNIL
BrB (3′ RNA-seq)
nNIL — count HMM, fit_means (rrate=1e-8)
wideseq-thinned BRB counts


5
BrB-rtiger
BrB (3′ RNA-seq)
RTIGER port (rigidity=7)
wideseq-thinned BRB counts


6
BrB-atlas
BrB (3′ RNA-seq)
ATLAS — GOOGA-shadow gene-level GT (≥.95/≥.25, min 5 reads) → geometric-duration HMM
cassini pangene counts, per donor species



So the grid is 2 sources × 3 caller-slots = 6 tracks, drawing on 4 unique callers (binhmm is Skim-only, ATLAS is BRB-only, nNIL/RTIGER run on both).






NoteMarker-set provenance — the tracks run at different SNP densities



The three Skim tracks are not all on the same markers. Skim-binhmm runs on the full dense set of ~27.6 M teosinte-informative SNPs (2.0–4.0 M per chromosome; total 27,603,777), whereas Skim-nNIL / Skim-rtiger run on the much sparser ~52 K “SNP50K” set. These are two different marker sets with two different provenances:

The ~27.6 M panel (wideseq_ref) is a teosinte-informative SNP set — positions where teosinte (the donor) carries variation relative to B73 (the recurrent parent), so a read there is diagnostic of donor-vs-recurrent ancestry. That informativeness is the whole point of the panel; it is a reference/filter set, not sample genotypes. It is built from the Schnable et al. 2023 Zea pan-genome VCF by keeping the teosinte/Tripsacum + B73 samples and biallelic SNPs — the MAF ≥ 0.05 cutoff is merely the mechanism that selects the donor-informative (species-common) sites (bzeaseq/get_variants_from_schnable2023.sh). GATK CollectAllelicCounts tallies each skim/BRB sample’s reads at these positions — so the BRB count tracks (BrB-nNIL, BrB-rtiger) draw on the same panel; only Skim-binhmm keeps the full 27.6 M density.
The ~52 K SNP50K set is an intersection — not a genotyping array, and not merely a coarse thinning of the 27.6 M panel. Its unfiltered base is the BZea NIL BAMs → GATK joint genotyping (N. Tandukar) + Beagle imputation (196,120 SNPs); intersecting those genotyped sites with the 27.6 M teosinte-informative panel yields 51,991 SNPs (so the 50K is itself teosinte-informative, being a subset of the panel). The Skim-nNIL / Skim-rtiger counts are reads tallied at those ~52 K sites. Here the teosinte panel is the filter and the BZea genotyping is the base — full chain + paths in sawers-rellan-labs/BzeaSeq → docs/donor_id_jaccard.md (“SNP50K & marker-set provenance”).

The MAF ≥ 0.05 filter keeps donor-informative (species-common) sites, which raises the donor dose and widens REF/HET/ALT separation — see genotype_likelihoods_and_hmm §5 and snp50k_genotype_identifiability §2.1/§2.6/§3.



Parameters are illustrative, not fully re-calibrated. This note is a coherence check across sources and callers, not a final call set.



1. Setup — engine, paths, samples


Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(nilHMM)
  library(data.table)
  library(ggplot2)
})
# Inputs are staged locally under data/ by stage_sanity_check_paint.R (see DATA.md),
# organized by (source x input type); paths below point into that layout.
SS <- here::here("data/skimsweep") # the vary-skim cohort
SKIM <- file.path(SS, "skim") # skim counts (counts_50k/) + bins (bins/)
BRB <- file.path(SS, "brb") # brb counts (counts_wideseq/) + pangene (pangene/)
REF <- here::here("data/ref") # genome-level ATLAS gene maps

# samples: control + 10 vary-skim NILs (ordered by skim lambda)
mem <- fread(file.path(SS, "coverage_sweep_members.csv"))[sweep == "vary_skim"][order(vary_cov)]
mem[, tax := sub("\\..*", "", nil)]
tax2dir <- c(
  Zx = "mexicana", Zl = "luxurians", Zd = "diploperennis",
  Zv = "parviglumis", Zh = "huehuetenangensis"
)
mem[, adir := tax2dir[tax]]
# wrapped left-hand label: cov + FULL pedigree string, then skim/brb plate ids
wraplab <- function(cov, ped, skim, brb) sprintf("%s\n%s\nskim:%s\nbrb:%s", cov, ped, skim, brb)
rows <- rbind(
  data.table(
    rowlab = wraplab("B73 ctrl", "B73 (control)", "PN10_SID893", "PN3_SID213"),
    skim = "PN10_SID893", brb = "PN3_SID213", adir = "mexicana"
  ),
  mem[, .(
    rowlab = wraplab(sprintf("%.2f×", vary_cov), nil, skim_name, brb_name),
    skim = skim_name, brb = brb_name, adir
  )]
)




2. ATLAS: competitive-mapping ancestry (GOOGA-shadow)
ATLAS = Ancestry by Transcript-Level Alignment Scores.
The basic principle is competitive mapping: count the reads that match each founder genome. Where the SNP-panel callers read an allele fraction at predefined variant sites, ATLAS instead aligns each transcript read against both founder references at once — B73 (the recurrent parent) and the teosinte donor transcriptome — and lets the alignment scores decide which parent that read came from. Per read, the best alignment score (with a margin k on the score gap ΔAS) votes the read as REF, DONOR, or AMBIGUOUS. A read that maps equally well to both parents landed on sequence that is identical between them (conserved — at ~2 % B73-vs-donor divergence this is ~98 % of positions), so it carries no ancestry information: AMBIGUOUS = non-informative → dropped (it is not a HET call). Summing the informative votes over a gene gives the two competitive-mapping counts, n_recur (B73-matching reads) and n_donor (donor-matching reads); ancestry is then just “which founder’s genome captured the reads at this gene.”
Caveat — the “donor” reference is a species-level stand-in, not the actual donor accession. We do not have the genome/transcriptome of the specific teosinte accession used in each cross, so the donor side of the competitive alignment is a representative reference for the donor’s species (per tax2dir: mexicana, luxurians, diploperennis, parviglumis, huehuetenangensis). A read is scored donor-vs-B73 against that proxy, not against the true parent — the same reference-data gap as the SNP-panel accession ceiling (snp50k_genotype_identifiability §2.6): sequence the accession carries but the species proxy lacks (or vice versa) is mis-scored, so n_donor is only as good as the stand-in’s match to the real donor.
GOOGA is the template. GOOGA (Fishman lab, PLoS Genet 2024, pgen.1011072) is a Mimulus F2 genetic-map-building pipeline whose ancestry step is threshold-then-HMM. ATLAS is a “GOOGA-shadow”: it ports that step onto the shared nilHMM engine —

Per-gene hard call from the count fractions (GOOGA s2.py, verbatim): n_recur + n_donor < 5 → missing; recurrent fraction ≥ 0.95 → REF (AA); donor fraction ≥ 0.95 → ALT / donor-hom (BB); both parents ≥ 0.25 → HET (AB); otherwise missing. (Thresholds 0.95 / 0.25, min 5 reads.)
Place each gene at its B73 coordinate and order it along the chromosome.
Smooth with the engine’s categorical genotype-error (gt) emission + geometric-duration HMM — the same duration/rigidity layer as every other caller, but a categorical confusion emission instead of count/BetaBinomial.

Why categorical and not a count model: these are 3′ RNA (BRB-seq) reads, so a gene’s read depth is driven by expression, not coverage, and allele-specific expression pushes a true HET’s donor fraction off 0.5 — both confound a count/BetaBinomial emission. Discretizing to a hard genotype first (exactly what GOOGA, itself an eQTL/ASE pipeline, does) makes the emission expression-agnostic. Memory: cassini-googa-caller, feedback-reproduce-from-source.
The code below is the consumer-side staging: read the cassini pangene counts per donor species, apply the GOOGA hard call, map genes to B73 coordinates, and decode each chromosome through the gt-emission geometric-duration HMM.


Show code
g2p <- fread(file.path(REF, "gene_to_pangene.tsv"))[species == "B73", .(gene, pan_gene)]
co <- fread(file.path(REF, "b73_gene_coords.tsv"),
  header = FALSE,
  col.names = c("gene", "chr", "start", "end")
)
gmap <- merge(g2p, co, by = "gene")
gmap[, chr := as.integer(sub("chr", "", chr))]
gmap <- unique(gmap[!is.na(chr)], by = "pan_gene")[, .(pan_gene, chr, pos = end)]
# The GOOGA hard-call (`≥.95`/`≥.25`, min 5 reads) + gt/geometric-duration HMM
# are the packaged ATLAS caller (`call_ancestry(caller = "atlas")`). Here we do
# only the consumer-side staging — read pangene counts, map to B73 gene
# coordinates — and hand the engine `(name, chr, pos, n_ref = recurrent,
# n_alt = donor)`. `rrate = 1e-3` matches the geometric duration used originally.
atlas_seg <- function(brb, adir) {
  f <- file.path(BRB, "pangene", adir, paste0(brb, ".pangene_counts.tsv"))
  if (!file.exists(f)) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  ct <- merge(fread(f), gmap, by = "pan_gene")[order(chr, pos)]
  seg <- as.data.table(call_ancestry(
    ct[, .(name = brb, chr, pos, n_ref = n_recur, n_alt = n_donor)],
    caller = "atlas", design = "BC2S3", rrate = 1e-3
  ))
  seg[, .(chr, start_bp, end_bp, state)]
}




3. binhmm (corrected) on the dense skim bins
3-state Gaussian-emission HMM on per-bin ALT_FREQ, porting the nilhifi chromosome_painting.R fix: REF is anchored at the baseline floor (mean fixed, sd from the low bins) so REF noise stays REF; HET/ALT are seeded from the data and refined by hard-EM with REF fixed; sticky transitions + design start priors + collapse guards. This replaces the free K=3 GMM (which always invents a 3rd cluster → HET over-call).


Show code
# The anchored 3-state Gaussian HMM is the packaged `binhmm` gauss backend. Our
# input is the pre-computed per-1 Mb `bin_genotypes.tsv` (ALT_FREQ over the dense
# ~27.6 M set); `call_ancestry(caller = "binhmm")` accepts a pre-binned table (an
# `alt_freq` column + `start_bp`/`end_bp`, `pos` as the bin order key) and runs
# the HMM directly, with no re-binning.
binhmm_gauss <- function(skim, design = "BC2S2") {
  f <- file.path(SKIM, "bins", paste0(skim, "_bin_genotypes.tsv"))
  if (!file.exists(f)) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  d <- fread(f)
  obs <- data.table(
    name = skim, chr = as.integer(sub("chr", "", d$CONTIG)), pos = d$BIN_POS,
    alt_freq = d$ALT_FREQ, start_bp = d$BIN_START, end_bp = d$BIN_END
  )
  seg <- as.data.table(call_ancestry(obs, caller = "binhmm", design = design))
  seg[, .(chr, start_bp, end_bp, state)]
}




4. Count / RTIGER via the engine
nNIL and RTIGER are the same engine, different duration. Both are count-emission callers: they read per-SNP allele counts and score each marker with the count / BetaBinomial emission through the one nilHMM engine. The only thing that separates them is the duration layer — nNIL uses a geometric duration (a free per-marker switch probability), RTIGER a rigidity layer (an integer minimum run length). So both are just call_ancestry(read_counts(path), caller = …) with different arguments; there is no separate RTIGER program and no Julia.
The duration knob has a distinct name per caller (no overloaded r): caller = "nnil" takes rrate, the expected per-marker recombination rate of the geometric duration (Holland’s nNIL calibrates it as 2·total_cM / (100·n_markers) ≈ 30/n_markers for a 1500 cM maize map, and it is tunable from data by KS-vs-sim); caller = "rtiger" takes rigidity, the integer minimum run length (e.g. 5). (nilHMM’s rigidity duration also has an exit rate xrate, but the faithful RTIGER port doesn’t use it.) The four count tracks below (Skim-nNIL, Skim-RTIGER, BrB-nNIL, BrB-RTIGER) differ only in caller, design prior, and these knobs — assembled in §5.
eng() is the thin adapter that wires a count file into the engine: locate and read the counts, decode through call_ancestry(), and hand back just the four columns the paint needs.


Show code
# Locate a sample's count TSV under `dir`. Recursive because NILs and the B73
# control may sit in different subfolders; returns NA if the sample isn't staged.
find1 <- function(dir, s) {
  f <- list.files(dir, paste0("^", s, "\\.tsv$"), full.names = TRUE, recursive = TRUE)
  if (length(f)) f[1] else NA
}
# Read a count file and decode it through the shared engine with the caller +
# params in `...` (caller, design, rrate (nnil) / rigidity (rtiger), err, …).
# Keep only the paint's 4 columns; return NULL — skipped, not fatal — if the
# file is missing, so one absent sample never aborts the sweep.
eng <- function(path, ...) {
  if (is.na(path) || !file.exists(path)) {
    return(NULL)
  }
  seg <- as.data.table(call_ancestry(read_counts(path), ...))
  seg[, .(chr, start_bp, end_bp, state)]
}
SK50 <- file.path(SKIM, "counts_50k") # skim 50K-panel counts   (Skim-nNIL, Skim-RTIGER)
BRBW <- file.path(BRB, "counts_wideseq") # BRB wideseq-thinned counts (BrB-nNIL, BrB-RTIGER)




5. Assemble all tracks


Show code
methods <- c(
  "Skim-binhmm", "Skim-nNIL", "Skim-rtiger",
  "BrB-nNIL", "BrB-rtiger", "BrB-atlas"
)

# run a caller, returning NULL (with a message) instead of aborting on error
grab <- function(fn) {
  tryCatch(fn, error = function(e) {
    message("  ! ", conditionMessage(e))
    NULL
  })
}

all <- list()
for (i in seq_len(nrow(rows))) {
  rl <- rows$rowlab[i]
  sk <- rows$skim[i]
  br <- rows$brb[i]
  ad <- rows$adir[i]

  sk_counts <- find1(SK50, sk) # skim 50K panel counts
  brb_counts <- file.path(BRBW, paste0(br, ".tsv")) # wideseq-thinned BRB counts

  tr <- list(
    `Skim-binhmm` = grab(binhmm_gauss(sk)),
    `Skim-nNIL` = grab(eng(sk_counts,
      caller = "nnil", design = "BC2S2", rrate = 1e-4, err = 0.01
    )),
    `Skim-rtiger` = grab(eng(sk_counts, caller = "rtiger", rigidity = 5L)),
    `BrB-nNIL` = grab(eng(brb_counts,
      caller = "nnil", design = "BC2S3", rrate = 1e-8, fit_means = TRUE
    )),
    `BrB-rtiger` = grab(eng(brb_counts, caller = "rtiger", rigidity = 7L)),
    `BrB-atlas` = grab(atlas_seg(br, ad))
  )

  # keep the non-empty tracks, tagging each with its row label and method
  for (m in names(tr)) {
    if (!is.null(tr[[m]]) && nrow(tr[[m]])) {
      all[[length(all) + 1L]] <- tr[[m]][, `:=`(rowlab = rl, method = m)]
    }
  }
}
df <- rbindlist(all)
df[, .N, by = method]


        method     N
        <char> <int>
1: Skim-binhmm   231
2:   Skim-nNIL   290
3: Skim-rtiger   265
4:    BrB-nNIL   223
5:  BrB-rtiger   329
6:   BrB-atlas   290




6. Paint


Show code
yb <- data.table(method = methods, k = 5:0) # top -> bottom
df <- merge(df, yb, by = "method")
df[, `:=`(ylo = k / 6 + 0.01, yhi = (k + 1) / 6 - 0.01)]
# B73 ctrl on top, then skim-cov descending (facet switch = 'y')
df[, rowlab := factor(rowlab, levels = c(rows$rowlab[1], rev(rows$rowlab[-1])))]
df[, chr := factor(chr, levels = 1:10, labels = paste0("chr", 1:10))]
df[, state := factor(state, 0:2, c("REF", "HET", "ALT"))]
pal <- c(REF = "gold", HET = "springgreen4", ALT = "purple4")
p <- ggplot(df, aes(xmin = start_bp / 1e6, xmax = end_bp / 1e6, ymin = ylo, ymax = yhi, fill = state)) +
  geom_rect() +
  facet_grid(rowlab ~ chr, scales = "free_x", space = "free_x", switch = "y") +
  scale_fill_manual(values = pal, drop = FALSE) +
  scale_y_continuous(
    breaks = (5:0) / 6 + 1 / 12, labels = methods, position = "right",
    expand = expansion(mult = 0.01)
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "nilHMM sanity check paint — vary-skim sweep (B73 control + 10 NILs)",
    subtitle = "6 tracks = data source × nilHMM caller (2 sources: Skim, BrB | 4 callers: binhmm, nNIL, RTIGER, ATLAS) | ordered by source then caller | all nilHMM, no Julia",
    x = "Position (Mb)", fill = "Genotype"
  ) +
  ggpubr::theme_classic2(base_size = 8) +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(size = 12),
    plot.subtitle = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.position = "bottom",
    legend.text = element_text(size = 12),
    legend.title = element_text(size = 12),
    panel.spacing.x = unit(0.04, "lines"),
    panel.spacing.y = unit(0.15, "lines"),
    strip.background = element_blank(),
    strip.text.y.left = element_text(
      angle = 0, hjust = 1, size = 12, lineheight = 0.9
    ),
    axis.text.y.right = element_text(size = 11),
    axis.ticks.y = element_blank(),
    axis.title.x = element_blank(),
    axis.text.x = element_blank(),
    axis.ticks.x = element_blank()
  )
dir.create(here::here("results"), showWarnings = FALSE)
ggsave(here::here("results/nilhmm_sanity_check_paint.png"), p, width = 16, height = 11, dpi = 130, limitsize = FALSE)
fwrite(df, here::here("results/nilhmm_sanity_check_paint_segments.tsv"), sep = "\t")
p











7. Sanity read

The anchor holds: the B73 control (top row) is essentially all-REF across all six tracks — a clean control paints clean.
NILs show localized donor blocks that broadly agree across callers. The major introgressions land in the same chromosomes across independent callers and across the two data sources — the core sanity result.
Caller-specific behavior, as expected: Skim-binhmm paints the broadest blocks and is more permissive toward ALT; BrB-rtiger is the noisiest (the known BRB-RTI RNA false-donor floor); BrB-atlas and BrB-nNIL are the most conservative.

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text: Date: 2026-07-03 (updated after fixing a determinism bug in rtiger_fit_cpp) Machine: Apple Silicon (arm64), 10 cores, macOS (Darwin 25.5); R 4.5. Reference implementation: RTIGER R package (Julia backend via juliaup), rHMM_methods.jl. Test implementation: nilHMM::call_ancestry(caller = "rtiger") — a C++ (Rcpp) port of the RTIGER fork in src/rtiger.cpp / R/rtiger.R. Data: the real SNP50K BZea NIL counts staged in the zealtiger repo (data/rtiger_50K/): per-taxon cohorts of counts/<donor>/<sample>.tsv, the 51,991-SNP teosinte-informative panel. Taxa Zh (n=61) and Zl (n=121). Reference RTIGER calls/fits: calls_taxa_r8.csv, fits_taxa*/<taxon>/rtiger_result.rds.

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
Summary
nilHMM’s rtiger reproduces RTIGER faithfully on both Zh and Zl: identical optimizer, identical convergence criterion, and — after fixing a determinism bug found during this validation — the same converged emission and the same iteration count. Three findings underpin this, each verified against the actual Julia source and the saved RTIGER fits (not from code comments):

Same optimizer. nilHMM’s EM is a line-for-line C++ port of RTIGER’s Julia Baum–Welch: identical getlogpsi / productpsi / forward / backward / Viterbi kernels, and the identical emission M-step (closed-form BetaBinomial mean + univariate Brent on the precision, same bracket).
Same convergence criterion — a delta, not a deviance. Both stop on round(max(|Δα|,|Δβ|), 6) ≤ eps with eps = 0.01 (RTIGER’s Julia source, line 1547, and its R wrapper default; confirmed against a run’s fit_progress.log). An earlier draft of this note said RTIGER used a “deviance” criterion — that was wrong; the EMdev/dev() function is unused.
Same preprocessing. RTIGER decodes covered markers only (~12–16 k of 51,991 per sample) and requires ≥ 2·rigidity covered markers per chromosome, aborting otherwise. nilHMM now matches both (see fixes below).

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
Correctness







taxon / comparison
result




Zh — identical params + identical covered markers (decode of RTIGER’s exact fit)
100.00% per-marker; ALT 4387/4387 (recall & precision 1.000)


Zh — nilHMM’s own r=8 fit + covered filter vs RTIGER
99.95% per-marker; donor-present Dice 1.000; ALT recall 0.965


Zl — nilHMM’s own r=8 fit vs RTIGER (emission means, QC’d)
19 iterations, means 0.99 / 0.870 / 0.659 vs RTIGER 19 iters, 0.99 / 0.870 / 0.661


Zh — nilHMM’s own r=5 fit vs RTIGER (per-marker, 848 k)
99.76%; ALT recall 0.944 / precision 0.802


Zl — nilHMM’s own r=5 fit vs RTIGER (per-marker, 1.98 M)
99.95%; ALT recall 0.997 / precision 0.989




Given the same parameters and marker support, the two Viterbis are bit-for-bit identical, including the rare donor-hom state.
With nilHMM doing its own fit, it now converges to RTIGER’s emission on both taxa — Zl matches iteration-for-iteration (19) and mean-for-mean (to ~2e-3, i.e. float-summation / Brent-tolerance noise). The residual on Zh (0.05% of markers) is HET↔︎ALT boundary jitter on the rare, overdispersed donor-hom state.
Note: RTIGER’s own Zl fit is a poorly-identified optimum (states at 0.99/0.87/0.66, barely separated — Zl sits on a flat likelihood ridge). nilHMM now lands on that same ridge point rather than wandering off it.

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
section: The determinism bug (found and fixed during this validation)
text:
The determinism bug (found and fixed during this validation)
An earlier draft claimed nilHMM’s fit was “deterministic (seeded).” It was not. On Zl, repeated identical calls (same data, seed, threads) returned different iteration counts (1, 2, 3, 8…) and different converged means — sometimes returning the init unchanged after “1 iteration.”
Root cause, traced through src/rtiger.cpp:

A chain (sample × chromosome) with fewer than 2·rigidity covered markers has an empty zeta window; its E-step collapses to exp(-Inf − (-Inf)) = NaN, which poisons the pooled sufficient statistics. The emission M-step’s NaN fallback then silently kept the init, reporting a bogus 1-iteration “convergence.”
The E-step was always dispatched through RcppParallel parallelFor, even at threads = 1 (there was no real serial path). parallelFor did not reliably process the whole range across repeated calls, so it nondeterministically skipped the degenerate chains — masking the NaN on some calls and not others.

Fixes (src/rtiger.cpp):

Real serial path for threads ≤ 1 — the worker is called directly, not through parallelFor. Deterministic; threads > 1 keeps the parallel fold.
Hard stop on under-covered chains (< 2·rigidity), matching RTIGER’s Julia abort — up front on the main thread, with a clear message — instead of silently skipping or NaN-ing. A totw guard remains as a backstop for empty input.

Verified after the fix: QC’d input is deterministic across threads = 1 and threads = 4 and repeated calls (all 19 iterations, means 0.99/0.870/0.659, matching RTIGER); under-covered input hard-stops with "rtiger: chain N has M covered markers, below the 2*rigidity = K floor …".

min_cov (covered-marker preprocessing)
call_states/call_ancestry gained min_cov on the rtiger path (default 1L): markers with n_ref + n_alt < min_cov are dropped before fit/decode, matching RTIGER’s covered-only decoding. min_cov = 0L restores decoding every marker.

At matched threads = 4, nilHMM is consistently ~30% faster than the Julia RTIGER across the whole rigidity grid.

On the earlier “1.4× slower” numbers. An earlier draft reported nilHMM at ~1.2–1.9× slower. That was a machine-contention artifact: the pre-fix sweep ran alongside other jobs, inflating nilHMM’s times (r=2 was 36.2 s under load vs 14.5 s quiet) while Julia’s stayed ~21 s. On a quiet machine at matched threads, nilHMM wins. Timing is load-sensitive; single-run, single-machine.

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
nnil vs rtiger (same covered support)
With min_cov applied to both callers, nnil and rtiger now decode the identical covered-marker set, so their calls join directly, per-marker, with no support mismatch. This is a caller-vs-caller concordance (there is no ground truth on real data; rtiger is the reference, itself validated against Julia RTIGER above). Taxon Zh (61 samples, 848,498 covered markers), design = "BC2S2".

Reading it — the two are different models and agree to ~98%:

Soft (nnil) vs hard (rtiger) segmentation. rtiger’s rigidity is a hard minimum run length — it structurally cannot emit a donor block shorter than r markers. nnil’s rrate is a soft per-marker switch prior — it only discourages short blocks. So nnil admits a larger tiny-block tail (40 vs 18 sub-0.1 Mb blocks): single covered markers with a spurious donor read open a 1-marker block that rtiger forbids. (Raising nnil’s err toward the real per-read error rate absorbs these; see the fragmentation exploration.)
3-state breakpoints go the other way — nnil 484 < rtiger 743. rtiger’s extra transitions are HET↔︎ALT flips within donor regions; nnil’s duration prior smooths those, so it fragments the donor state less even while admitting a few more tiny donor blocks.
rrate is the fragmentation knob for nnil: 1e-4 → 465 blocks (median 9.9 Mb); 1e-3 → 705 blocks (median 5.4 Mb), matching rtiger’s block count less well. Neither rrate nor err was calibrated here — these are illustrative defaults, not an optimized operating point.
Speed is equal on the covered support (~1.0 s each for 61 samples); both benefit from the min_cov filter (nnil ~4× faster than on the full panel).

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
Performance (engineering, measured during this work)
Parallelism and amortization added to nilHMM while validating against RTIGER — each verified to preserve exact output:

Per-sample parallelism. call_states’ per-sample loop (emission fit + decode) is independent across samples; it now runs via parallel::mclapply (threads) with an rbindlist assembly. fit_means on Zl (121 samples): 45 s → 13.4 s (3.3×) at threads = 8, bit-identical output.
to_segments vectorized. Replaced the per-(name, chr) RLE loop + do.call(rbind) with one O(n) pass (order by group+pos; a run starts wherever the group or state changes). On 1.98 M markers: ~6.5 s → 1.3 s (5×), bit-identical. Speeds every caller and both sweep modes.
caller_sweep() — fit-once parameter scan. The segmentation parameter (rigidity/rrate) is a prior, not an emission property, so the emission is fit once (at ref) and the grid is swept by decodes fanned across threads. On a 12-rigidity Zl grid (threads = 8): 10.4 s vs 37.1 s cold (3.6×); the advantage grows with grid size and shrinks when the grid fits in cores (cold’s fits parallelize). Workflow: scan with refit = "none" → pick the best value → take the exact final calls from one call_ancestry() at that value.

refit = "none" (fit once, reuse) is exact at ref and a close approximation elsewhere: mean seg-overlap-to-cold 0.93, but min 0.62 at the grid extremes — keep ref central and the grid not too wide.
refit = "cold" refits per value (exact baseline; == per-value call_ancestry).
A warm-start mode was evaluated and dropped: on the multimodal rtiger fit it reaches a different optimum than cold, so it is neither exact nor better than none for ranking (warm seg-overlap-to-cold 0.94–0.96, below none’s 0.97–0.99, and slower).


Two dead ends, recorded for honesty: an is.data.frame “hotspot” in the fit_means EM was a profiling artifact (fixing it changed nothing; reverted), and the warm-start’s “exact but cheaper” premise fails on multimodal fits (above).

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
Reproduce
Scripts (session scratchpad; data paths point at the zealtiger repo):

time_sweep.R — head-to-head timing (JULIA_NUM_THREADS=4 Rscript time_sweep.R).
definitive.R / covered.R — identical-input → 100% per-marker check (Zh).
endtoend.R — nilHMM’s own-fit + covered filter vs RTIGER (Zh, 99.95%).
qc_det.R — determinism across threads + Zl emission match to RTIGER (post-fix).
iters_zl.R — EM iteration counts, nilHMM vs RTIGER’s saved fits.
nnil_vs_rtiger.R — nnil vs rtiger on the shared covered support (Zh).
bench_sweep.R / cmp_refit.R — caller_sweep vs cold timing + refit-mode fidelity.
tseg_check.R — to_segments correctness (vs old RLE) + speed.

title: RTIGER reproduction & timing: nilHMM rtiger vs the Julia RTIGER
text:
Conclusion
nilHMM’s rtiger is a faithful reproduction of RTIGER — identical optimizer, kernels, and delta convergence criterion; exact calls on identical input; and, after the determinism fix, the same converged fit and iteration count on both Zh and Zl, at r = 5 and r = 8 (~99.8–99.95% per-marker). The two behavioural gaps to RTIGER are now closed: decoding all markers (fixed by min_cov) and the under-covered-chain NaN/nondeterminism (fixed by the serial path + 2·rigidity hard stop). On a quiet machine at matched threads, nilHMM’s rtiger is ~30% faster than the Julia RTIGER across the rigidity grid. Beyond parity, the engineering pass (per-sample mclapply, a 5× to_segments, and the fit-once caller_sweep) makes cohort fits and rigidity/rrate scans several-fold cheaper again.

title: Cross-modality concordance on the paired BrB + skim cohort
text:
The benchmark cohort is the ~400 NILs that have BOTH BRB-seq and skim data. The paired design is the point: the same NIL genotype seen through two modalities, so cross-source disagreement is method, not sample. We apply the sim-calibrated parameters from simulation-calibration.qmd and quantify concordance per sample.

Emits (paper): Figure F4 (400-cohort source × method concordance), Results §3.3. Chromosome painting appears here only as supplementary QC (see nilhmm_sanity_check_paint.qmd), never as a main figure.







ImportantSTATUS: blocked (B5 open input)



Needs the ~400 paired-cohort sample manifest (IDs, donor species, per-source depth). Trace from sample_metatada.csv / memory bzea-sample-donor-metadata plus the BRB and skim sample maps; the vary-skim sweep members are a subset. Record it in DATA.md before running. This note is the analysis harness that consumes that manifest.




Show code
suppressMessages({
  library(nilHMM)
  library(data.table)
  library(ggplot2)
})
for (f in list.files(here::here("R"), "\\.R$", full.names = TRUE)) source(f)
mnt <- bzea_mounts()



1. The three source paths (calibrated params from B2.2)

Skim (low-cov WGS + TP teosinte–B73 wideseq filtering): nNIL, RTIGER, binhmm.
BrB (3′ RNA-seq + TP filtering): nNIL, RTIGER, ATLAS.
Target-seq (MolBreeding 45K, high coverage): gt path — completeness only, and doubles as the optional Holland-style calibration cross-check; not headline.



2. Concordance metrics (per sample)
Computed on a shared marker/bin grid so tracks with different breakpoints are comparable (helpers in R/metrics.R):

pairwise segment agreement — pairwise_agreement()
donor-footprint Jaccard — donor_jaccard()
breakpoint concordance — compare breakpoint_count() across tracks
control cleanliness on B73 — control_donor_rate() (should be ~0)

# jac <- donor_jaccard(skim_calls, brb_calls, grid)
# agr <- pairwise_agreement(skim_calls, brb_calls, grid)




3. Read (to write once data is in)
Cross-source / cross-caller concordance distributions, and where the modalities systematically disagree (expected: BRB-RTIGER RNA false-donor floor; ATLAS most conservative). This is F4.

title: TeoNAM composite genetic map (bcsft(1,4), B73 v5)
text:
What this is. A results view of the composite TeoNAM genetic map — the native per-marker cM (cm) built by scripts/teonam_qtl_permap.R → scripts/teonam_qtl_map.R (per-family maps → union → Chen-2019 flanking imputation → bcsft(1,4) est.map, markers ordered on B73 v5). That build takes 5+ hours, so this notebook only loads the finished map and renders it — it runs no est.map.
The map is island-free on chr7: the v2→v5 displaced block (6 markers isolated by ~5 cM gaps) was caught by the connected-component quirky finder (scripts/map_tools.R) and removed, collapsing that gap 10.22 → 0.76 cM and chr7 154.5 → 144.85 cM (diagnosis in the QTL-recovery notebook).
Show code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)

  v <- z$cm - min(z$cm)
  names(v) <- z$marker

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) [118K panel]
text:
What this is. The STAM GWAS Manhattan (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) reproduced on the TeoNAM genotypes, with the domestication candidate genes placed at their real B73 v5 coordinates (MaizeGDB genemodel_locus → b73_gene_coords), a red star at each gene’s genomic position, and the LOD = 5 dotted significance line.
This notebook loads pre-computed CSVs and plots (compute lives in the scripts/ directory): the per-marker GWAS scans (teonam_stam_gwas_scan.R interpolated / teonam_stam_gwas_family_imputed.R family-imputed) and the candidate-gene overlap (teonam_candidate_overlap.R). We show both the family-imputed scan (natural scatter) and the interpolated/densified scan (block-terraced — the artifact of step-interpolated genotypes, see below).
Show code
library(here)
library(data.table)
library(ggplot2)
library(fastman)
library(ggrepel)
library(ggtext)
library(scales)
library(cowplot)
Show code
# Coordinate transformer replicating fastman_gg's internal x layout, so we can
# place gene labels / lines at arbitrary (chr, bp). From inv4m make_manhattan_plots.Rmd.
get_transformer <- function(m) {
  cmat <- do.call(rbind, lapply(
    split(m[order(m$CHR, m$BP), ], m$CHR[order(m$CHR, m$BP)]),
    function(d) {
      data.frame(
        CHR = d$CHR[1], min_bp = min(d$BP),
        width = max(d$BP) - min(d$BP),
        medgap = if (nrow(d) > 1) median(diff(sort(d$BP))) else 1
      )
    }
  ))
  cmat <- cmat[order(cmat$CHR), ]
  maxgap <- max(cmat$medgap, na.rm = TRUE)
  numc <- nrow(cmat)
  cmat$base <- 0
  cmat$midp <- 0
  cmat$midp[1] <- cmat$width[1] / 2
  for (i in 2:numc) {
    cmat$base[i] <- cmat$base[i - 1] + cmat$width[i - 1] + maxgap
    cmat$midp[i] <- cmat$base[i] + cmat$width[i] / 2
  }
  fac <- numc / cmat$midp[numc]
  cmat$basef <- fac * cmat$base
  function(chr, bp) {
    i <- match(chr, cmat$CHR)
    fac * (bp - cmat$min_bp[i]) + cmat$basef[i]
  }
}

LOD <- 5 # Chen 2019: P < 1e-5 (LOD = 5)

#' STAM GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes at their true v5 positions.
#' Odd chromosomes black, even gray75; y-axis expanded one major-tick unit above
#' the top tick; gene labels repelled into the headroom above the maximum peak
#' (segments back to the red star at each gene's local peak).
#' @param scan_csv per-marker scan (SNP, CHR, BP[v5], P)
#' @param title    plot title
#' @param out_png  optional path to save a PNG
plot_stam_manhattan <- function(scan_csv, title, out_png = NULL, mark = NULL) {
  scan <- if (is.data.frame(scan_csv)) as.data.frame(scan_csv) else as.data.frame(fread(scan_csv))
  scan <- scan[is.finite(scan$P) & scan$P > 0, ]
  m <- scan[order(scan$CHR, scan$BP), c("SNP", "CHR", "BP", "P")]

  ov <- fread(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_candidate_overlap.csv")) # symbol, chr, start (v5)
  # star height = local max -log10P within +/-500 kb IN THIS scan
  ov[, y := vapply(seq_len(.N), function(i) {
    w <- m$P[m$CHR == chr[i] & abs(m$BP - start[i]) <= 5e5]
    if (length(w)) -log10(min(w)) else NA_real_
  }, numeric(1))]
  tr <- get_transformer(m)
  ov[, BPn := tr(chr, start)]
  ov <- ov[is.finite(y)]

  mp <- -log10(min(m$P)) # maximum peak
  ticks <- pretty(c(0, mp))
  unit <- diff(ticks)[1]
  ytop <- max(ticks) + unit # one major-tick unit of headroom

  p <- fastman_gg(
    m = m, snp = "SNP", col = c("black", "gray75"), maxP = mp,
    genomewideline = NULL, suggestiveline = NULL,
    ylab = expression(-log[10](italic(P))), xlab = ""
  ) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = LOD, linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.7, color = "black") +
    geom_point(
      data = ov, aes(x = BPn, y = y), inherit.aes = FALSE,
      shape = 8, size = 3, stroke = 1, color = "red"
    ) +
    ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
      data = ov, aes(x = BPn, y = y, label = symbol),
      inherit.aes = FALSE, fontface = "italic", size = 5,
      segment.color = "grey50", min.segment.length = 0, box.padding = 0.5,
      max.overlaps = Inf, ylim = c(mp + 0.3 * unit, ytop)
    ) + # above the max peak
    ggtitle(title) +
    theme_classic(base_size = 18) +
    scale_y_continuous(
      breaks = ticks, limits = c(0, ytop),
      expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0))
    ) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_markdown(hjust = 1, size = 16, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "none",
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.2)
    )

  if (!is.null(mark)) { # optional dashed marker at an arbitrary locus
    xm <- tr(mark$chr, mark$bp)
    p <- p + geom_vline(xintercept = xm, linetype = "dashed", color = "red", linewidth = 0.5)
  }
  if (!is.null(out_png)) ggsave(out_png, p, width = 9, height = 4.6, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
  p
}

#' Chen 2019 Fig 4A: JLM QTL genomic distribution as a lollipop plot.
#' Full 10-chromosome axis is obtained by passing the dense scan to fastman_gg
#' with fully transparent points (col = transparent); the JLM QTL are overlaid as
#' blue stems + points, candidate genes labeled bold-italic above.
#' @param jlm_txt  TASSEL StepwiseOLSModelFitter output (stam_jlm_native1.txt): Name,
#'   Locus, Position (v5 bp), `pr>F`
#' @param scan_csv dense per-marker scan, only used (transparent) to lay out the axis
plot_stam_lollipop <- function(jlm_txt, scan_csv, title, out_png = NULL) {
  scan <- as.data.frame(fread(scan_csv))
  scan <- scan[is.finite(scan$P) & scan$P > 0, ]
  maxis <- scan[order(scan$CHR, scan$BP), c("SNP", "CHR", "BP", "P")]

  j <- fread(jlm_txt)
  j <- j[!(Name %in% c("mean", "Family", "Error"))]
  q <- data.table(
    SNP = j$Name, CHR = as.integer(j$Locus), BP = as.integer(j$Position),
    logP = -log10(as.numeric(j$`pr>F`))
  )
  q <- q[logP >= LOD] # keep only QTL significant at LOD 5 (-log10P >= 5)
  ov <- fread(here("results/sim/teonam/stam_candidate_overlap.csv")) # symbol, chr, start (v5)
  ov <- ov[symbol %in% c("ts2", "tb1", "tru1", "zmm16", "tsh4")] # JLM Fig-4A candidates (an1 is GWAS-only)
  q[, gene := ""] # assign each candidate gene to its NEAREST JLM QTL (1:1)
  for (i in seq_len(nrow(ov))) {
    idx <- which(q$CHR == ov$chr[i])
    if (length(idx)) {
      k <- idx[which.min(abs(q$BP[idx] - ov$start[i]))]
      q$gene[k] <- ov$symbol[i]
    }
  }
  tr <- get_transformer(maxis)
  q[, BPn := tr(CHR, BP)]

  maxlogP <- max(q$logP)
  brks <- seq(0, floor(maxlogP / 10) * 10, by = 10)
  ytop <- maxlogP * 1.18 # headroom for the top label

  p <- fastman_gg(
    m = maxis, snp = "SNP", col = c("#00000000", "#00000000"),
    maxP = maxlogP, genomewideline = NULL, suggestiveline = NULL,
    ylab = expression(-log[10](italic(P))), xlab = ""
  ) +
    geom_segment(
      data = q, aes(x = BPn, xend = BPn, y = 0, yend = logP),
      inherit.aes = FALSE, color = "blue", linewidth = 0.5
    ) +
    geom_point(
      data = q, aes(x = BPn, y = logP), inherit.aes = FALSE,
      color = "blue", size = 2.5
    ) +
    ggrepel::geom_text_repel(
      data = q[gene != ""], aes(x = BPn, y = logP, label = gene),
      inherit.aes = FALSE, fontface = "bold.italic", size = 5,
      segment.color = NA, box.padding = 0.4, point.padding = 0.3,
      nudge_y = 0.05 * maxlogP, seed = 1, max.overlaps = Inf
    ) +
    ggtitle(title) + theme_classic(base_size = 18) +
    scale_y_continuous(breaks = brks, limits = c(0, ytop), expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0))) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_markdown(hjust = 1, size = 16, face = "bold"),
      legend.position = "none",
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.2)
    )
  if (!is.null(out_png)) ggsave(out_png, p, width = 9, height = 4, dpi = 200, bg = "white")
  p
}

#' Coverage-sweep line-Manhattan (geom_line, one line per coverage).
#' −log10P vs genomic position along the union grid; `group = interaction(CHR,
#' coverage)` so lines never connect across chromosomes. Color = coverage on a
#' viridis ramp, the λ = ∞ truth baseline in black. LOD = 5 dotted. NO gene labels.
#' Reused for the control / nNIL / RTIGER panels of the degradation composite.
#' @param sweep_csv combined sweep CSV (SNP, CHR, BP[v5], P, coverage; coverage=Inf
#'   for the λ=∞ baseline rows)
#' @param title panel title (markdown)
#' @param legend show the coverage legend (FALSE for composite panels sharing one)
#' @param base_size theme base size
plot_sweep_line <- function(sweep_csv, title, legend = TRUE, base_size = 14) {
  sw <- as.data.table(fread(sweep_csv))
  sw <- sw[is.finite(P) & P > 0]
  sw[, logP := -log10(P)]
  # x layout from the union grid (every coverage carries the identical marker set)
  tr <- get_transformer(as.data.frame(sw[coverage == sw$coverage[1], .(CHR, BP)]))
  sw[, BPn := tr(CHR, BP)]

  # Legend order: infinity (truth) at top, then coverage in descending order.
  cov_levels <- c("∞", "20", "10", "5", "1", "0.5", "0.2", "0.1")
  sw[, cov_lab := factor(ifelse(is.finite(coverage), formatC(coverage), "∞"),
    levels = cov_levels
  )]
  # Colour mapping is fixed by coverage value (0.1 = darkest viridis ... 20 =
  # lightest; ∞ = black), independent of the legend order above.
  cov_asc <- c("0.1", "0.2", "0.5", "1", "5", "10", "20")
  pal <- setNames(grDevices::hcl.colors(7, "viridis"), cov_asc)
  pal["∞"] <- "black"
  axis_df <- sw[, .(mid = (min(BPn) + max(BPn)) / 2), by = CHR][order(CHR)]
  ytop <- 20 # shared y-limit across all panels for cross-method comparison

  # Draw order (z-stack): 20x first, then descending to 0.1x, and ∞ LAST so the
  # darker low-coverage / black-truth profiles sit on top. dord = -coverage sorts
  # finite coverage descending (20 -> -20 first); ∞ -> Inf sorts last.
  sw[, dord := ifelse(is.finite(coverage), -coverage, Inf)]

  ggplot(
    sw[order(dord, CHR, BP)],
    aes(x = BPn, y = logP, color = cov_lab, group = interaction(CHR, coverage))
  ) +
    geom_line(linewidth = 0.4) +
    geom_hline(yintercept = LOD, linetype = "dotted", linewidth = 0.7, color = "grey30") +
    scale_color_manual(values = pal, name = "coverage (×)", drop = FALSE) +
    scale_x_continuous(breaks = axis_df$mid, labels = axis_df$CHR, expand = c(0.01, 0)) +
    scale_y_continuous(limits = c(0, ytop), expand = expansion(mult = c(0.01, 0))) +
    labs(x = "chromosome", y = expression(-log[10](italic(P))), title = title) +
    theme_classic(base_size = base_size) +
    theme(
      plot.title = element_markdown(hjust = 0, size = base_size, face = "bold"),
      panel.grid.major.y = element_line(color = "grey90", linewidth = 0.2),
      # legend INSIDE the plotting area, top-right corner (top panel only)
      legend.position = if (legend) c(0.995, 0.97) else "none",
      legend.justification = c(1, 1),
      legend.background = element_rect(fill = scales::alpha("white", 0.7), colour = NA),
      legend.key.size = unit(0.85, "lines"),
      legend.title = element_text(size = base_size - 3),
      legend.text = element_text(size = base_size - 4),
      legend.margin = margin(1, 3, 1, 3)
    ) +
    guides(color = guide_legend(override.aes = list(linewidth = 1.2)))
}

title: TeoNAM STAM — GWAS Manhattan with candidate genes (Chen 2019 Fig 4C) [118K panel]
section: Candidate genes: closest marker vs most-significant marker (v5)
text:
Candidate genes: closest marker vs most-significant marker (v5)
For each STAM candidate gene (B73 v5), two different “nearby marker” choices from the authentic 118,514-marker 118K GWAS scan (OLS): the closest marker (smallest bp distance to the gene body) and the window-max marker (highest −log₁₀P within ±500 kb — a 1 Mb window centered on the gene). They are not the same marker at any gene: the closest marker sits essentially on the gene but carries little signal, while the significant local signal is a nearby but distinct marker. This is the reference for what “near the gene” means in the Fig 4A lollipop below (which labels each gene onto its nearest JLM QTL).


Show code
sc <- fread(here("data/teonam/stam_gwas_scan_118k.csv"))
sc <- sc[is.finite(P) & P > 0][, logP := -log10(P)]
cand <- c(
  ts2 = "STAM1.1", tb1 = "STAM1.2", tru1 = "STAM3.1",
  zmm16 = "STAM3.2", tsh4 = "STAM7.1", an1 = "GWAS(chr1)"
)
sym <- fread(here("data/ref/maize_v5_gene_symbols.tsv"))
setnames(sym, c("gene_id", "symbol"))
coord <- fread(here("data/ref/b73_gene_coords.tsv"),
  header = FALSE,
  col.names = c("gene_id", "chr", "start", "end")
)
gg <- merge(sym[symbol %in% names(cand)], coord, by = "gene_id")
gg[, chr := as.integer(sub("^chr", "", chr))][, qtl := cand[symbol]]
WIN <- 5e5 # +/-500 kb around the gene body (1 Mb window)
ctab <- rbindlist(lapply(seq_len(nrow(gg)), function(i) {
  ch <- gg$chr[i]
  s <- gg$start[i]
  e <- gg$end[i]
  m <- sc[CHR == ch]
  d <- pmax(0L, pmax(s - m$BP, m$BP - e))
  kc <- which.min(d) # closest to gene body
  w <- m[BP >= s - WIN & BP <= e + WIN]
  kw <- which.max(w$logP) # max signal in window
  data.table(
    gene = gg$symbol[i], QTL = gg$qtl[i], chr = ch, `gene start (v5)` = s,
    `closest Δstart (bp)` = m$BP[kc] - s, `closest -log10P` = round(m$logP[kc], 2),
    `window-max Δstart (bp)` = w$BP[kw] - s, `window-max -log10P` = round(w$logP[kw], 2)
  )
}))
setorder(ctab, chr, `gene start (v5)`)
knitr::kable(ctab, format.args = list(big.mark = ","), caption = paste(
  "STAM candidate genes (B73 v5): the CLOSEST marker vs the highest -log10P marker",
  "within +/-500 kb (authentic 118K GWAS scan, 118,514 markers). Δstart = marker bp -",
  "gene start (v5), signed (negative = upstream); the two markers differ at every gene."
))
